driver_rates.name: 10-Year Treasury Yield
driver_rates.explanation: Benchmark for 30-year fixed mortgage pricing. When the 10Y rises, mortgages follow within days.

driver_rates.name: 30-Year Treasury Yield
driver_rates.explanation: Long-end of the curve. Direct sentiment proxy for very long-duration debt like mortgage-backed bonds.

driver_rates.name: 2-Year Treasury Yield
driver_rates.explanation: Front-end of the curve — most sensitive to Fed expectations. Moves first when policy outlook shifts.

driver_rates.name: 5-Year Treasury Yield
driver_rates.explanation: Belly of the curve. Indexes some hybrid ARMs and reflects the medium-term rate path.

driver_rates.name: Federal Funds Rate
driver_rates.explanation: The Fed's policy rate. Sets the floor for short-term borrowing and bleeds into mortgage pricing via expectations.

driver_rates.name: SOFR (Overnight Funding)
driver_rates.explanation: Successor to LIBOR. Indexes most new ARMs and floating-rate commercial mortgages.

driver_rates.name: 10Y Breakeven Inflation
driver_rates.explanation: Market-implied 10-year inflation from TIPS spreads. Rising breakevens lift nominal yields and mortgage rates.

driver_rates.name: MBS Current-Coupon Spread
driver_rates.explanation: 30-yr mortgage minus 10-yr Treasury. The mortgage industry's own risk premium — wider spread = costlier mortgages.

related_indicators.name: S&P 500
related_indicators.explanation: Risk-on equity rallies pull capital out of bonds and lift mortgage rates.

related_indicators.name: CPI YoY
related_indicators.explanation: Headline inflation. Hotter prints push the Fed hawkish and lift the long end of the curve.

related_indicators.name: Core PCE YoY
related_indicators.explanation: The Fed's preferred inflation gauge — drives policy expectations more than CPI.

related_indicators.name: CPI Rent YoY
related_indicators.explanation: Shelter inflation. The single largest CPI component — the Fed watches this closely for sticky-inflation signals.

related_indicators.name: Unemployment Rate
related_indicators.explanation: Labor slack. Higher unemployment usually means lower mortgage rates as Treasury yields fall.

related_indicators.name: Nonfarm Payrolls (Δ k)
related_indicators.explanation: Monthly change in U.S. jobs. The headline jobs print — surprises move 10Y yields the fastest.

related_indicators.name: Initial Jobless Claims (k)
related_indicators.explanation: Weekly read on layoffs. Rising claims often precede rate cuts as the labor market softens.

related_indicators.name: JOLTS Job Openings (k)
related_indicators.explanation: Open jobs across the economy. A leading wage-and-inflation signal the Fed weights heavily.

related_indicators.name: Real GDP YoY
related_indicators.explanation: Overall growth. Strong growth + tight labor = sticky rates.

related_indicators.name: Personal Saving Rate
related_indicators.explanation: Households' saving rate. Lower saving = stretched consumers and weakening housing demand.

related_indicators.name: Housing Starts (SAAR, k)
related_indicators.explanation: Supply pipeline. Affects home prices more than rates, but feeds Fed thinking on shelter inflation.

related_indicators.name: Building Permits (SAAR, k)
related_indicators.explanation: Forward-looking supply signal — permits lead starts by a few months.

related_indicators.name: New Home Sales (SAAR, k)
related_indicators.explanation: New-construction demand. Highly rate-sensitive — buyers walk fast when financing costs rise.

related_indicators.name: Existing Home Sales (SAAR, k)
related_indicators.explanation: Demand-side housing health — declines often precede rate cuts.

related_indicators.name: Months Supply (New Homes)
related_indicators.explanation: Months of new-home inventory at current pace. >6 = buyer's market, <4 = seller's.

related_indicators.name: Case-Shiller Home Prices YoY
related_indicators.explanation: National home price growth. Higher prices = larger loan sizes and more interest-rate-sensitive monthly payments.

related_indicators.name: Mortgage Delinquency Rate
related_indicators.explanation: Single-family delinquencies at all commercial banks. A late-cycle signal: rising delinquencies precede credit tightening.

key_indicators.name: 10Y–2Y Yield Spread
key_indicators.explanation: Difference between the 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields. A negative spread (inverted curve) has preceded most U.S. recessions.

key_indicators.name: Trade-Weighted Dollar Index
key_indicators.explanation: Broad measure of the dollar vs. major trading partners. A strong dollar imports disinflation and tends to cap U.S. rates.

key_indicators.name: VIX Volatility Index
key_indicators.explanation: S&P 500 30-day implied volatility. Spikes coincide with flight-to-quality bond buying, which pulls mortgage rates lower.

key_indicators.name: Consumer Sentiment
key_indicators.explanation: University of Michigan survey. Forward-looking proxy for spending, housing demand, and inflation expectations.

key_indicators.name: M2 Money Stock ($B)
key_indicators.explanation: Broad money supply. The liquidity backdrop for everything — outsized expansion fed the 2020–2022 inflation surge.

key_indicators.name: Fed Balance Sheet ($B)
key_indicators.explanation: Total assets held by the Federal Reserve. Expansion (QE) compresses term premia and mortgage spreads; runoff (QT) widens them.

key_indicators.name: Fed MBS Holdings ($B)
key_indicators.explanation: Mortgage-backed securities on the Fed's balance sheet. Direct supply/demand for MBS — drives the mortgage-Treasury spread.

key_indicators.name: High-Yield Credit Spread
key_indicators.explanation: ICE BofA HY OAS. Wider spreads = risk-off; capital flows into Treasuries, pulling mortgage rates down with them.

key_indicators.name: 3-Month T-Bill
key_indicators.explanation: Front-end policy proxy. Diverges from the funds rate when the market is pricing imminent Fed action.

key_indicators.name: 5y5y Forward Inflation
key_indicators.explanation: 5-year inflation expectations starting 5 years out. The Fed's favorite long-term inflation anchor — drives the rates outlook.
